variation_instruction.prompt: IMPORTANT: Implement from the inside out. Use the wishful programming approach. Write the internal code you wish you had, before you write the code that uses it.

llm_gate_results.reasoning: The implementation faithfully covers all explicit requirements: (1) 5-minute snapping is implemented via snapStep in difficulties.js (levels 1-3 use snapStep:5, level 4 uses snapStep:1) with angleToMinutes()/snapToStep() utilities applied during drag; (2) Mouse and touch interactions are handled in AnalogClock via touchstart/touchmove/touchend with preventDefault to prevent scroll interference and touch-action:none in CSS; (3) SentenceClock uses Pointer Events API (unified mouse+touch) with drag threshold detection, ghost element, and double-tap within 350ms window; (4) Responsive UI with 540px breakpoint, CSS grid/flexbox, CSS variables for consistent theming; (5) Reusable components (AnalogClock, DigitalClock, SentenceClock classes) and utility modules (time.js, zinnen.js, dom.js); (6) Placeholder tests in test/snap.test.js and test/touch.test.js covering snapping logic and touch interaction behavior; (7) No comments or documentation in source files. The implementation goes beyond a literal reading by also handling hour-wrap logic during minute dragging, providing a complete game flow with difficulty levels, and using proper SVG coordinate transformation for precise hand interaction.

Both test files contain meaningful assertions that verify real behavior. snap.test.js tests snapToStep with concrete input/output pairs, angleToMinutes with exact expected values, and difficulty snapStep configuration. touch.test.js tests SentenceClock DOM rendering, double-tap state machine logic with time thresholds, slot placement, and slot removal — all with substantive expect() assertions. No tests are trivially passing without assertions, no existing tests appear to have been removed or weakened, and the tests exercise actual implementation logic rather than being placeholder stubs.

All visual changes are well-implemented: minute tick marks added to analog clock with proper subtle styling (opacity 0.4, stroke-width 1), word-slot and word-block heights updated consistently from 40px to 44px, drag ghost element properly styled with fixed positioning and z-index 9999 matching word-block appearance, and drag feedback opacity improved from 0.4 to 0.7 for active state. No occlusion, misalignment, overflow, or layout breakage detected in the code changes.

variation_instruction.prompt: IMPORTANT: Think through the entire plan 2 times. Ask what could go wrong, and plan again. Only then should you start implementing.

llm_gate_results.reasoning: The implementation faithfully addresses all explicit requirements: (1) Minute hand snapping to 5-minute multiples is implemented via snapTo() in time.js and snapMinutes option in AnalogClock.js — difficulty levels 2 and 3 set minuteSnap:5 while level 4 (free) uses snap:1; (2) Touch/mobile interactions use the Pointer Events API with setPointerCapture() for analog clock dragging, ensuring smooth continuous tracking on touchscreens; (3) SentenceClock implements double-tap detection (<300ms), drag-and-drop with ghost element, and hit testing via elementsFromPoint() for mobile-friendly word placement; (4) The UI uses a responsive grid layout, breakpoints at 540px, CSS custom properties for consistency, and clamp()-based fluid typography — achieving a minimal, tight, consistent feel; (5) Reusable components (AnalogClock, SentenceClock, DigitalClock, app-card, app-shell) and utility modules (time.js, dom.js, zinnen.js, store.js) demonstrate good code organization; (6) Multiple test files (time.test.js, difficulties.test.js, store.test.js, dom-utils.test.js, zinnen.test.js, app-card.test.js) run via vitest with 'npm run test'. The implementation captures the underlying purpose — a progressive clock-reading practice app with accessible mobile-first interactions — not just a literal reading of the spec.

Both new test files (time.test.js and difficulties.test.js) contain real, meaningful assertions. time.test.js tests all exported utility functions (pad2, hoursToAngle, minutesToAngle, angleToMinutes, angleToHours12, pointAngle, handEndpoint, snapTo) with specific expected values and edge cases like wrapping behavior. difficulties.test.js tests MINUTE_LEVELS, HOUR_MODES, and getDifficulty with concrete assertions about structure, values, and behavior. No tests are trivially passing without assertions, no existing tests appear to have been removed or weakened, and the tests verify actual intended behavior.

The UI changes are limited to interaction handling (pointer events replacing drag & drop) and minor CSS additions. The new .word-ghost element uses position:fixed with z-index:9999 and pointer-events:none, correctly layering above content without causing occlusion. Ghost cleanup is properly handled on pointerup and pointercancel. touch-action:none additions are non-visual. No layout structure, typography, or spacing was changed. No visual defects identified through code analysis.

Tournament scores:

comparisons.reasoning: B reduces incidental complexity in two key areas: (1) AnalogClock uses the unified Pointer Events API (pointerdown/pointermove/pointerup/pointercancel with pointer capture) instead of separate mouse and touch handler pairs, cutting event-handling code roughly in half with no loss of functionality; (2) _renderMarkers loops 12 times instead of 60, eliminating 48 unnecessary DOM elements and the logic that distinguished hour from minute markers. The snapTo utility in B adds a minor guard condition (snap <= 1) that A avoids, but this is outweighed by the structural simplifications above. SentenceClock differences are roughly a wash — B removes _tapState and some helpers but adds _hitTest. Net result: B has fewer constructs that exist only to support other introduced constructs.
